items.name: Notification Channel Volatility
items.rationale: How the community is reached changes continuously as new channels emerge (email, SMS, push, chat apps) and existing ones deprecate. SR-6, SR-15a, and SR-15b demand that channel implementations evolve independently of the engagement logic they serve, and Objective 3 requires onboarding within one release cycle. This is open-ended change in THIS customer's external ecosystem over time, not a fixed enumeration a conditional could handle, so it must be encapsulated behind the notification dispatcher.

items.name: Identity Provider Volatility
items.rationale: The external party that vouches for who a member or organizer is will change as the platform adopts new identity providers or migrates between them. SR-10, SR-11, and Objective 1 require that member and organizer relationships remain constant even as the underlying provider changes. The set of providers is open-ended and their protocols differ unpredictably over time, so the identity broker must encapsulate this rather than branching on provider type.

items.name: Venue Data Source Volatility
items.rationale: External venue data sources are swapped and added over the platform's life as integrations come and go. SR-12, SR-13, and SR-19 require mediating between an event and external venue information such that sources can be sourced or changed without altering the event's structure. This is enduring, open-ended change in the external data ecosystem, encapsulated behind the venue resolver.

items.name: Engagement Mechanism Volatility
items.rationale: What constitutes engagement (RSVP, comment, reminder, and future forms of participation nurturing) evolves as the platform discovers better ways to translate intent into attendance. SR-8, SR-9, SR-17, and SR-20 together with Objective 2's conversion target imply that the orchestration of engagement mechanisms is a moving target over time, not a fixed set. Encapsulating it centrally keeps the engagement logic stable as its constituent mechanisms grow.

items.name: Event Lifecycle Definition Volatility
items.rationale: The defined lifecycle states an event passes through from draft to conclusion change as the organizing model matures (new intermediate states, approval gates, archival). SR-1 and SR-22 require governing transitions and auditing them. The shape of the lifecycle is open-ended for this customer over time, so the organizing engine must encapsulate the lifecycle definition rather than hard-coding a single state sequence.

items.name: Reminder Scheduling Policy Volatility
items.rationale: SR-7 references the reminder schedule in an external policy document (v1.0, Section 3.2), and policy documents are revised. How and when members are reminded is a business rule that changes over time as organizers learn what drives attendance. This is genuine policy volatility, not a one-off conditional, and must be encapsulated so the engagement orchestration can adopt new reminder policies without disruption.

items.name: Access and Authorization Policy Volatility
items.rationale: Which roles may read or mutate event, engagement, identity, and venue information—and which mutations are sensitive—changes as the community's governance model and trust boundaries evolve. SR-21 enumerates today's rules, but role definitions and protected operations are open-ended over the platform's life. Encapsulating the authorization policy prevents access rules from being scattered across components.

items.name: Event Discovery Criteria Volatility
items.rationale: How members discover events available to them (relevance, locality, interest matching) varies by community type and market, and differs across customer segments today. SR-4 states the capability but the rules that determine which events are surfaced to whom are not done identically by all communities and shift as the connected-community vision matures. This open-ended discovery logic warrants encapsulation behind the organizing engine.

items.name: Identity Vouching Semantics Volatility
items.rationale: Different markets and customer types rely on different identity providers with materially different vouching semantics, attribute sets, and assurance levels at one time. SR-10 and SR-18 require recognizing members and organizers regardless of provider while retaining the enduring identity association. Because providers vary across the customer base simultaneously, the identity broker must encapsulate these differences behind a uniform identity relationship abstraction.

items.name: Venue Information Semantics Volatility
items.rationale: Across communities and regions, venue data sources differ in structure, availability semantics, and the very meaning of a 'place' (physical address, virtual link, hybrid) at the same point in time. SR-12 and SR-13 require mediating these external differences without altering the event's structure. Since the variation exists across customers concurrently, the venue resolver must encapsulate the heterogeneous venue semantics.
